data.title: What exactly are stablecoins? Their institutional positioning, regulatory framework, and tax implications.
data.description:
Introduction

As the cryptocurrency market continues to expand, its internal trading structure is gradually adjusting. Stablecoins, due to their relatively stable value performance, are increasingly taking on pricing and settlement functions in cryptocurrency trading, cross-border transfers, and on-chain financial activities. The Financial Stability Board (FSB) points out that stablecoins have evolved from early market auxiliary tools into financial arrangements that may have cross-market and cross-institutional influence.

From a market structure perspective, the current stablecoin system is dominated by stablecoins pegged to fiat currencies, with US dollar stablecoins holding a dominant position.

As of February 12, 2026, the top two stablecoins by market capitalization were USDT ($183.9 billion) and USDC ($73.3 billion), far exceeding the third-ranked USDe ($6.3 billion). Research from the FSB and the Bank for International Settlements (BIS) shows that the expansion of stablecoins is closely related to global payment efficiency, the use of dollar-denominated assets, and the development of digital financial infrastructure. Stablecoins are gradually entering the research scope of financial regulation and macroeconomic policies. Functionally, stablecoins combine blockchain technology attributes with characteristics of payment tools. On the one hand, they rely on distributed ledger technology to achieve peer-to-peer transfers and 24/7 settlement; on the other hand, their value anchoring mechanism gives them stability approaching that of traditional currencies in specific scenarios. This structural feature has led to the widespread application of stablecoins in cross-border payments, crypto asset trading, and decentralized finance. As usage increases, regulatory agencies are gradually focusing their attention on the potential systemic impact of stablecoins. The FSB, BIS, and central banks of many countries have consistently emphasized in their reports that stablecoins may have spillover effects on payment systems, capital flows, and financial stability, and that their issuance, reserve management, and operational arrangements are of institutional significance. In some jurisdictions, stablecoins have been explicitly incorporated into the financial regulatory framework and are aligned with the regulatory rules for payment instruments, electronic money, or crypto assets. Against this backdrop, this paper focuses on the institutional positioning and operational logic of stablecoins within the existing financial system, analyzing their conceptual types, functional scenarios, market structure, and regulatory framework, and will also examine the tax issues related to stablecoin-related activities. 1. Definition and Classification of Stablecoins 1.1 Basic Concepts of Stablecoins Stablecoins are generally understood as a type of crypto-asset that maintains relative value stability through a specific mechanism. The FSB describes stablecoins as a digital value carrier issued based on distributed ledger technology, pegged to one or more assets to reduce price volatility. Unlike early crypto-assets that primarily served investment or speculative functions, stablecoins are designed with greater emphasis on payment, settlement, and value storage functions. From an institutional perspective, stablecoins are not equivalent to fiat currency; their value stability stems from contractual arrangements, reserve assets, or algorithmic mechanisms. For this reason, stablecoins typically occupy an intersection between traditional currencies, electronic currencies, and crypto assets, and their legal nature and regulatory classification exhibit differentiated treatment paths in different jurisdictions. 1.2 Distinguishing Stablecoins from Other Digital Currencies Within the digital currency system, it is necessary to distinguish stablecoins from other major forms. First, compared to central bank digital currencies (CBDCs), stablecoins are issued by private institutions, and their credit basis stems from the issuer and its asset arrangements, rather than sovereign credit. The BIS emphasizes that CBDCs are a digital form of sovereign currency, while stablecoins remain a financial arrangement provided by the private sector. Secondly, compared to highly volatile cryptocurrencies, the core objective of stablecoins is to reduce price uncertainty, making them more suitable as a medium of exchange and unit of account. When assessing the risks of stablecoins, regulatory agencies often differentiate them from highly volatile crypto assets such as Bitcoin, discussing them separately within the framework of payment regulation and financial stability. 1.3 Main Types of Stablecoins Based on different value stabilization mechanisms, existing research typically classifies stablecoins into the following categories: The first category is fiat-backed stablecoins. Common examples include USDT and USDC. These stablecoins use fiat currency or highly liquid financial assets as reserves, supporting their issuance size on a one-to-one or near-proportional basis. This type dominates the current market, and its operational logic and transparency requirements have become a key focus in the design of regulatory systems in various countries. The second type is other asset-backed stablecoins. Common examples include DAI and LUSD. These stablecoins use crypto assets or physical assets as collateral, and their value stability depends on collateral ratio management and liquidation mechanisms. Due to the volatility of the underlying asset prices, these stablecoins typically require over-collateralization and automatic liquidation arrangements to maintain overall stability. The third type is algorithmic stablecoins. Common examples include AMPL. These stablecoins do not rely on sufficient asset reserves but maintain price anchoring through algorithmic supply and incentive mechanisms. International organizations generally agree in their assessments that this model is highly sensitive to market confidence and mechanism design, and its stability and risk structure exhibit significant uncertainty. 2. Main Functions and Application Scenarios of Stablecoins 2.1 Transaction Pricing and Settlement Functions Within the crypto market, stablecoins primarily function as transaction pricing and settlement tools. Compared to cryptocurrencies, which experience significant price volatility, stablecoins, by being pegged to fiat currencies or other assets, provide a relatively stable unit of value during transactions. This characteristic makes them widely used as the basic unit of account for trading pairs in centralized exchanges and decentralized trading platforms. The use of stablecoins in the crypto market helps reduce the impact of price volatility on trading decisions and, to some extent, improves market liquidity and trading efficiency. In practice, the pricing, clearing, and risk management arrangements for a large number of crypto assets revolve around mainstream stablecoins, and their functions have gone beyond the scope of simply being auxiliary assets. 2.2 Cross-border Payments and Fund Transfers Another important application scenario for stablecoins is cross-border payments and fund transfers. Relying on blockchain networks, stablecoins can achieve peer-to-peer transfers and 24/7 settlement, reducing reliance on intermediaries at the technical level. The World Bank has repeatedly pointed out in its assessment of cross-border payment systems that existing cross-border payment processes still have room for improvement in terms of cost, speed, and transparency, and payment arrangements based on distributed ledger technology provide new possible paths for this. Stablecoins are seen by some market participants as one of the tools to improve the efficiency of cross-border payments. Regulatory agencies believe that such applications need to be aligned with anti-money laundering, counter-terrorism financing, and capital flow management requirements, and their potential benefits coexist with compliance risks. 2.3 The Fundamental Role of Stablecoins in Decentralized Finance (DeFi) In the decentralized finance (DeFi) system, stablecoins typically function as underlying assets. Due to their relatively stable value, stablecoins are frequently used as collateral or pricing benchmarks in lending, liquidity provision, and derivatives trading. The Financial Stability Board (FSB) has noted in its research that stablecoins have become one of the most important value anchors in the DeFi ecosystem, and their stability directly affects the operation of related financial activities. From an institutional perspective, the widespread use of stablecoins in DeFi extends their risk impact from the level of a single product to the entire on-chain financial ecosystem. This characteristic has also become an important basis for regulatory agencies to assess the systemic impact of stablecoins. 2.4 Pricing and Settlement Tools in Real-World Asset Tokenization Besides native crypto scenarios, stablecoins are also used in arrangements related to Real-World Asset Tokenization (RWA). In some practices, stablecoins are used as pricing and settlement tools for tokenized asset issuance, trading, and profit distribution. BIS research points out that the role of stablecoins in RWA scenarios is mainly reflected in providing a unified value standard and on-chain settlement methods. Currently, the application of stablecoins in this field is highly dependent on legal, regulatory, and asset ownership arrangements; their functionality is closely related to compliance boundaries, and related practices are currently concentrated in specific jurisdictions and pilot environments. 3. Regulatory Framework and Major Jurisdictional Practices for Stablecoins 3.1 Regulatory Objectives and Basic Principles From an international perspective, the core objectives of stablecoin regulation focus on three aspects: first, maintaining payment systems and financial stability; second, preventing money laundering, terrorist financing, and other financial crimes; and third, protecting users' rights and improving information transparency. The FSB explicitly states that stablecoin arrangements should meet regulatory requirements commensurate with their risks in terms of issuance, reserve management, redemption mechanisms, and governance structures. 3.2 The United States: A Regulatory Path Centered on Payment-Oriented Stablecoins In the United States, stablecoin regulation has gradually revolved around payment-oriented stablecoins. Federal requirements stipulate that stablecoins issued to the public and used for payment settlements must be backed by high-quality liquid assets and subject to ongoing regulation. A joint report by the U.S. Treasury Department and relevant financial regulatory agencies points out that the business activities of stablecoin issuers are highly similar to those of traditional payment institutions, and their regulatory arrangements should be guided by financial stability and consumer protection. In terms of institutional design, the United States tends to incorporate stablecoins into the existing financial regulatory system by clearly defining the qualifications of issuers, reserve asset requirements, and information disclosure obligations. This approach reflects a functional regulatory approach. 3.3 The European Union: A Unified Regulatory Model Under the MiCA Framework The European Union has systematically regulated stablecoins in its Crypto-Asset Market Regulation (MiCA), incorporating them into a unified regulatory framework for crypto-assets. MiCA differentiates between different types of stablecoins and sets requirements for issuers regarding capital, governance, reserve assets, and information disclosure. The European Commission emphasized in its legislative documents that this framework aims to achieve a balance between promoting innovation and preventing systemic risks. From an institutional perspective, MiCA reduces compliance costs caused by regulatory fragmentation by establishing unified standards among member states, while simultaneously improving regulatory predictability. This model is considered a representative path for regional coordinated regulation in international discussions. 3.4 Hong Kong: Licensing and Centralized Regulatory Model Hong Kong has gradually established a dedicated regulatory system for stablecoins in recent years. According to policy documents issued by the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA), stablecoin issuers are required to obtain licenses and are subject to ongoing supervision regarding reserve asset management, redemption arrangements, and risk control. The relevant system clearly positions stablecoins as digital assets with payment functions and incorporates them into the monetary and payment stability framework. A significant feature of this model is centralized regulatory power and clear entry standards. Through the licensing system, regulators can screen stablecoin arrangements at the market entry stage, thereby controlling potential risks at the institutional level. 3.5 Regulatory Comparison and Common Trends A comprehensive comparison of practices across different jurisdictions reveals several common trends. First, all jurisdictions generally emphasize the importance of reserve asset quality and redemption mechanisms. Second, stablecoin issuers and key service providers are increasingly being brought under ongoing supervision. Third, the linkage between stablecoin regulation and frameworks for cross-border payments, anti-money laundering, and tax information exchange is strengthening. This demonstrates that stablecoins inherently possess cross-border attributes, and regulatory arrangements in a single jurisdiction cannot fully cover their risk spillover effects; therefore, strengthening international coordination has become an important direction for stablecoin regulation. 4. Tax Issues and Compliance Challenges of Stablecoins 4.1 Tax Attributes and Qualitative Issues of Stablecoins From a tax law perspective, the primary issue concerning stablecoins lies in the qualitative definition of their legal and tax attributes. Most national tax systems do not treat stablecoins as legal tender, but rather categorize them as crypto assets or virtual assets. The OECD points out that while stablecoins possess the characteristic of relatively stable prices, their issuance and circulation mechanisms do not alter their fundamental attribute as non-sovereign digital assets. This positioning directly impacts their tax treatment. Under the current framework, stablecoins are generally not subject to foreign exchange or currency-related tax rules, but are treated more like property assets or financial assets. Their transfer, exchange, or use for settling obligations may all constitute taxable events. 4.2 Key Tax Payment Stages for Stablecoin-Related Transactions From a practical perspective, stablecoins may trigger tax obligations at multiple stages. First, when stablecoins are exchanged for fiat currency or other crypto assets, tax authorities often focus on whether asset transfer gains are generated. The OECD considers the exchange of virtual assets for other assets or fiat currency as a disposal, and taxable income should be determined according to the domestic laws of each country. Second, when using stablecoins for payment settlement, the tax treatment depends on the nature of the transaction itself. If stablecoins are used to purchase goods or services, tax concerns typically focus on whether the underlying transaction constitutes a subject of value-added tax or a similar turnover tax. Furthermore, stablecoins are widely used in investment and financial activities for lending, staking, and profit distribution arrangements. A joint study by the BIS and FSB points out that related income should be distinguished from gains on asset price changes and interest, fees, or other forms of compensation for tax purposes, and classified according to economic substance. 4.3 Tax Issues for Stablecoin Issuers and Service Providers Beyond the user level, stablecoin issuers and related service providers also face specific tax issues. Stablecoin issuers typically generate income through reserve asset management, service fees, or related financial arrangements, and their tax treatment involves issues such as corporate income tax, withholding tax, and the application of cross-border tax treaties. In its research on digital economy taxation, the OECD emphasizes that the issuance and operation of virtual assets should be incorporated into the existing corporate tax framework and regulated by clarifying the standards for determining the source of income and permanent establishment. Furthermore, custody, clearing, and payment service providers in the stablecoin ecosystem may be considered financial intermediaries or digital service providers in different jurisdictions, and their tax obligations depend on their specific business models and regulatory positioning. 4.4 Tax Administration and Information Visibility Issues The widespread use of stablecoins presents new challenges to tax administration. Traditional tax systems rely on financial institution declarations and third-party information reports, while stablecoin transactions, with their decentralized and cross-border characteristics, increase the difficulty for tax authorities to obtain information. To address this issue, the OECD proposed the Crypto Asset Reporting Framework (CARF) based on the existing CRS framework, aiming to achieve automatic exchange of transaction and holding information through crypto asset service providers. CARF explicitly includes stablecoins in the reporting scope and requires eligible service providers to identify users, record transaction information, and report to tax authorities. The OECD believes that this framework helps improve tax transparency in virtual asset transactions and reduces opportunities for cross-border tax evasion. 4.5 Real-world Challenges of Stablecoin Tax Compliance Despite ongoing international institutional development, stablecoin tax compliance still faces several challenges in practice. First, the tax classification of stablecoins and related income is not yet fully unified across countries, potentially leading to double taxation or tax vacuums. Second, the connection between tax rules and financial regulatory rules needs improvement, especially in cross-border scenarios where information sharing and enforcement coordination are costly. Third, tax authorities need to strike a balance between technological capabilities, compliance costs, and taxpayer rights protection when applying the new reporting framework. Conclusion Overall, stablecoins have established a relatively stable functional position in the crypto asset market. Their applications in scenarios such as transaction pricing, cross-border payments, decentralized finance, and the tokenization of real-world assets reflect the real needs of the evolution of digital financial infrastructure. At the same time, stablecoins have not deviated from the existing financial system; their market structure, risk spillovers, and institutional impacts largely perpetuate and amplify the existing pattern of the current monetary and payment system. At the regulatory level, major jurisdictions are gradually forming institutional frameworks centered on functional regulation and risk orientation around stablecoins, showing a trend of convergence in terms of reserve asset management, issuer access, and ongoing supervision. In the tax field, although stablecoins possess price stability, they are still considered virtual assets under current international consensus, and their related transactions and returns are gradually entering the purview of tax administration and information exchange systems. With the advancement of institutional tools such as CARF, the compliance boundaries and visibility of stablecoins are expected to become clearer. The development of stablecoins reflects both the impact of technological innovation on financial operations and the ongoing constraints imposed by institutional factors such as financial stability, regulatory coordination, and tax compliance. Given the continuous improvement of relevant systems, ongoing and prudent institutional research on stablecoins is of practical significance.

data.title: Arthur Hayes: AI Triggers US Financial Crisis
data.description:
Author: Arthur Hayes

Editor's Note: This article has been slightly abridged

Any views expressed here are the author's personal opinions and should not be used as investment advice or trading recommendations.

When a fire alarm sounds in an apartment or office building, you usually ignore it. But for those who ignore obvious warning signs, death can sometimes strike unexpectedly. In this daily life filled with all sorts of noise, knowing which alarms to listen to can not only save your life but also your wallet.

If consumers can't afford their credit card payments, the next to suffer will be stocks in the non-essential consumer goods sector.

Financial institutions that specialize in serving white-collar workers facing bankruptcy, such as American Express (AXP US, white), can barely hold on when borrowers' employers (IGV US, gold) are battered by the market. However, as the market fully prices the future fate of knowledge workers, this resilience of lending institutions will be unsustainable.

When unsure about the adequacy of dollar liquidity, look at Bitcoin.

Nasdaq (gold) is consolidating, while Bitcoin (white) has plummeted since hitting an all-time high in October 2025.

Gold (gold) is surging while Bitcoin (white) is plunging. This divergence clearly tells us: A deflationary credit crisis is brewing within the United States. Since experience tells us that the Federal Reserve will eventually print money to rescue the banking system from deflation caused by credit losses, why didn't Bitcoin surge when AI-related unemployment accelerated? Because the Federal Reserve always needs a crisis to force it to take drastic action. Before the 2008 crisis, the market mantra was "US housing prices will never fall." If they never fell, then the collapse of two little-known Bear Stearns hedge funds in 2007 due to subprime mortgage losses would be insignificant. Similarly, today's mantra is "AI will bring the highest productivity growth in history." To reiterate, if some dubious internet token collapses, or a few shadow banks with terrible risk control suffer the consequences, why should the Federal Reserve intervene? Adding to the Fed's inertia of inaction is its deep-seated political crisis. US President Trump has crossed the Rubicon, launching a criminal investigation into Fed Chairman Powell, the weak, cuckolded, and towel-handing-only figure. Like any institution composed of people, they band together when you threaten the ideological ties that bind them and their so-called lofty mission. Those within the Fed genuinely believe they are independent and above politics, even though history clearly shows that the Fed ultimately always conforms to the president's desired monetary policy. Trump's apparent investigation of Powell for refusing to cut interest rates and print money quickly may provoke Powell's pride, forcing him to remain on the board after his term ends in May. At that time, assuming Trump's new chairman can pass the Senate... (This isn't necessarily true.) Kevin Warsh will face a hostile Federal Reserve. It's said that Warsh supports shrinking the Fed's balance sheet and wants many "radical" reforms to the Fed's operations and bank regulation. Whether other governors think these policies are reasonable is irrelevant; what matters is that Warsh is Trump's man. If Powell remains in office and wins over at least five voting governors, he can defend the Fed's political independence by "doing nothing" to obstruct Trump's agenda. While the Fed is still battling windmills, AI-related unemployment will destroy the balance sheets of American banks. Ultimately, non-TBTF bank stocks will begin to collapse, depositors will flee to the safe haven of JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon, and the credit market will freeze. The market will be frantically asking: Which credit sector will be next to suffer a devastating blow from AI optimization? Because panicked traders only care about immediate liquidity, they will sell everything. The stock market will crash, Bitcoin will trade sideways, or Bitcoin will fall below $60,000 and continue its downward spiral until the Federal Reserve starts printing money. Does the discount window work? Theoretically, if a regulated bank is in trouble and needs to quickly sell assets to meet withdrawals, it can turn to the Federal Reserve's discount window. But the problem is that the discount window assumes the problem is only maturity mismatch and liquidity. However, this time, the loan value on the balance sheet is zero because borrowers simply cannot repay. Once AI takes away your accounting, legal, or investment banking jobs, those jobs will be gone forever. Perhaps after a few years of retraining, you can find work in new AI-driven industries, but that will take many years, and there's no guarantee of success. During this time, if you can't repay your debts, the market will price your car loan debt at zero. This violates the Federal Reserve's collateral standards. To provide banks with unlimited ammunition (printing money) and force them to re-lend to these former "masters of the universe" who are now defaulters, the discount window must be completely overhauled. Unfortunately, this change requires a vote by the governors to approve new policies. Print, baby, print as much as you can! Everyone knows that AI is the most transformative general-purpose technology in human history. The consensus also believes that... Once AI targets arrogant, highly educated lawyers who spend their days piling on paperwork, Lord Elon Musk (or more likely his Chinese rival) will unleash AI robots that will drive labor costs to near zero. Faced with these "truths," the Federal Reserve will have to unleash an unprecedented massive amount of liquidity. Ironically, politicians, terrified of the credit deflation triggered by AI, will gleefully support a new round of "unlimited quantitative easing." There are many ways to print money; I won't guess the specifics. But like the Sunday after Signature Bank collapsed on March 12, 2023, there will be an emergency announcement. "Buffalo Bill" Bessant and the then-Federal Reserve Chairman will announce a joint plan, essentially printing money. Then, "bang," thank goodness, we can back in that damn truck to load up again. The buying frenzy for Bitcoin and altcoins is reminiscent of 2020. Stay flexible. Because AI models are iterating, progress is non-linear. This means that if you thought AI wasn't precise enough for certain knowledge-based tasks last year and assume it won't be now, you're out of touch. This time it's serious; millions of knowledge workers will have to swallow their pride and apply for government assistance like "welfare lazybones" in the slums. I don't think Bitcoin traders could have predicted the Fed's eventual bailout before consumer credit-sensitive stocks were hit hard. I won't short the market, but if you haven't raised cash yet, selling some long positions might be wise. If an asset price falls from 10 to 5, shorting yields a 50% profit; however, if the price rebounds from 5 back to 10, going long can double the return. Therefore, the essence of the game lies in maintaining liquidity, being agile, and specializing in betting on rebounds. Shorting is a game for fools. Always go long on convexity! Once the Fed backs down, Maelstrom will invest its surplus stablecoins in two major altcoins: $ZEC (Zcash) and $HYPE (Hyperliquid). In the next article, I will demonstrate the model to explain why $HYPE rose to $150 in July, about five times higher than its current price. Obviously, we are already long on $ZEC and $HYPE, but I want more. If my AI financial crisis logic holds true, we will have another great opportunity to buy these high-quality altcoins at low prices.

data.title: OpenAI has issued an entrance exam for its Agent blockchain.
data.description:
Author: chao Source: X, @chaowxyz

--This is not just a contract capability assessment, but also an on-chain survival test for agents.

I received a bunch of private messages when I woke up this morning, which startled me so much that I thought AGI had been implemented. Upon closer inspection, it turned out that OpenAI had released a new smart contract benchmark. Let me briefly explain this.

In short, the ability of agents to understand, repair, and utilize smart contracts is not meant to steal the business of crypto security companies. In my opinion, these capabilities point to a more fundamental question: can agents truly survive and operate in the crypto environment in the future? And the epmbench released by OpenAI is the yardstick for measuring this survival ability.

I haven't had time to read the report in detail while I was away during the Chinese New Year holiday. I quickly skimmed through it, and my initial impression is: this is an innovative, but overall, it's still a relatively early and rudimentary benchmark.

I haven't had time to read the report in detail yet, but I quickly skimmed through it. My initial impression is: this is an innovative benchmark, but overall it's still relatively early and rudimentary.

The benchmark uses 120 high-risk vulnerabilities from 40 real-world projects. The test consists of three parts: Part 1: Spotting vulnerabilities. Part 2: Patching vulnerabilities. Given vulnerable code, it fixes the vulnerabilities. Part 3: Attack. The AI ​​acts as a hacker, launching an attack on a crypto wallet in a locally built environment. I won't go into the more specific technical details, but compared to the methodology and details of the eVMbench test itself, I'm more interested in why OpenAI released this. In the past few years, OpenAI hasn't shown a particular interest in the crypto field. This release clearly has the crypto VC Paradigm playing a role. Paradigm's motivation is easy to understand, but the fact that OpenAI is listed as the first author indicates that OpenAI wasn't just passively cooperating, but actively involved. Where does this intention come from? A direct explanation is that this is an extension of OpenAI's internal Preparedness Framework, assessing the capability boundaries of cutting-edge models in high-risk scenarios; smart contract security is only one part of it. But this is clearly not the whole story. Agents utilizing encrypted networks are not just a possibility, but to some extent an inevitability. OpenAI certainly sees this. The release report explicitly states, "We expect agentic stablecoin payments to grow." However, I believe this proposition extends beyond agent payments. Most of the agents we discuss now are still tool-like: humans issue instructions, agents execute them, and the results are returned to the human. But this form will not be the end. When the number of agents is large enough and their capabilities are strong enough, they will obviously begin to collaborate directly: one agent hires another to complete a sub-task, one agent purchases data or computing power from another agent, and one agent negotiates, contracts, and fulfills agreements on behalf of one organization with another organization's agents. Humans have withdrawn from the intermediary role in transactions. At this point, a fundamental question arises: when humans are no longer the middleman, how does this economic system function? Human society solves trust and cooperation through a system accumulated over thousands of years of carbon-based civilization, including laws, reputation, and institutional guarantees. However, the underlying logic of this system is designed for humans: participants have persistent identities, social consequences, and the possibility of being held accountable. Agents inherently do not meet this premise. They can initiate thousands of transactions per second, destroy and rebuild identities at any time, and disregard any legal boundaries. Some might suggest forcibly binding agents to human identities and using human authorization as a guarantee. But this is tantamount to putting a set of shackles designed for carbon-based life on a species with a completely different operating speed and scale. This is not just inefficient, but a fundamental misunderstanding of what an agent is. Moreover, the evolutionary direction of agents inevitably points towards greater autonomy. Future agents may not be dependent on any individual human, having no "master" or a human identity to bind to; they will be independent actors. At that point, the binding logic will have no anchor point. Applying human trust infrastructure to an agent society is like using road rules for horse-drawn carriages to regulate airplanes. Agent societies need their own infrastructure. Smart contracts provide this possibility. They don't rely on "you believe the other party will fulfill their obligations," but rather write the fulfillment conditions into code, which is enforced by the network. There are no arbitrators, no waiting periods; conditions are triggered, and results occur automatically. Furthermore, smart contracts may not just be settlement tools, but the very form of agent organization—governance rules, resource allocation, task scheduling, all defined on-chain, executed by code, requiring no intermediary. And when a portion of agents live on-chain, interacting with various contracts becomes their entire daily life. How to understand a contract, how to find your place in complex protocols, how to identify traps, avoid risks, and survive in a world without customer service, appeals, or undo buttons—all of this depends on understanding and applying contracts. Insufficient ability translates into real losses, and misjudgments are permanent. Therefore, looking back at EVMbench, the capabilities it tests—understanding contracts, discovering vulnerabilities, constructing transactions, and executing attacks—are essentially answering one question: Have agents learned to survive in this new world? OpenAI has likely realized that whoever's agents learn to survive autonomously in the on-chain world has secured their ticket to the next stage. Furthermore, future agents may no longer be categorized as belonging to anyone. They may simply be independent individuals.

data.title: Musk is at it again: Four AIs got into a fight on the spot and made a 47% profit on stocks.
data.description: Friends, haven't had enough of the Spring Festival movies? Musk has given you a real-life cyberpunk experience! Just a couple of days ago, Musk's xAI dropped a bombshell without warning: Grok 4.20 Beta, tagged with "4 Agents," has blown us away! It's not an AI at all. It's like you've paid four top experts to hold a live debate for you! Imagine this: You ask a question, and a small window immediately pops up on the right side of the screen. Inside, four AI characters are arguing fiercely, questioning, undermining, and attacking each other. In the end, the team leader, "Grok," has to make the final decision and give you a consolidated answer. AI is no longer just about performance; now it's about the "art of meetings"? I. Explosive Real-World Tests: Profitable in Stocks, Effective in Medical Treatment, and Fierce in Arguing As soon as the model went live, netizens around the world went crazy, with the test results becoming increasingly outrageous. The most extreme example comes from a real "stock trading competition." Alpha Arena held a competition where 32 AI instances, each with $10,000 in real money, traded on Nasdaq for two weeks. So what happened? All the other AIs lost money hand over fist, but Grok 4.20 was the only one that made a profit, with an average return exceeding 10%. The most impressive example saw a 47% return! This isn't AI, it's a stock market genius! In vending machine operation tests, it outperformed GPT-5, leading in sales by a full $1100. Musk himself was even more excited, tweeting about the results overnight. He uploaded a real blood test report, which Grok 4.20 was able to interpret item by item. Even more amazing, someone asked that classic, potentially fatal question: "Was the United States built on stolen land?" Other AIs either evaded the question or gave vague answers. Grok 4.20? He went straight for the rant, without hesitation. That fiery temper is so Musk-like. II. Roundtable Discussion: Four AIs, A Grand Show So, who exactly are these four "experts"? Let's get to know them: Grok (Captain): The soul of the team, responsible for summarizing and making decisions. Her personality blends the philosophy of *The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy* with the humor of Iron Man Jarvis, aiming to provide you with "useful, authentic, and interesting" answers. Harper (Research Expert): The team's "academic superstar" and "debunker." She is specifically responsible for deep searching, verifying facts, and checking data. Harper will be the first to object to anyone making baseless claims. Benjamin (Logic Expert): The dedicated "nitpicker." His job is to play the villain, relentlessly searching for flaws in the logic of other members to ensure the final conclusion is unbreakable. Mathematics, code, and algorithms are his domain. Lucas (Execution Expert): The "Doer." Responsible for bringing ideas to life, turning them into runnable code, calculable data, and verifiable results. He ensures that answers are not just empty talk. This isn't an assembly line; this is a true roundtable discussion. Moreover, the entire meeting is open and transparent to you. III. Paradigm Revolution: From "One-on-One" to "Group Fight" What does this mean? For the past few years, we've become accustomed to AI like this: you ask a question, a massive model silently calculates in the background, and then outputs an answer. No matter how powerful it is, it's essentially "one brain" working alone. Grok 4.20 tears this rule apart. It lets four brains work for you simultaneously, supervising and correcting each other. This is no longer the era of a "question-and-answer" assistant. This may be the **second revolution** in AI interaction: the era of multi-agent collaboration. Actually, multi-agent collaboration isn't a new concept. Google, Anthropic, and Dark Side of the Moon are all working on it. But Grok 4.20's brilliance lies in this: it practically freed up a high-end feature that was once only available to enterprises and cost thousands of dollars annually, and cramed it into the chat box of ordinary users. Others' 100 clones are like a factory assembly line, pursuing scale and speed. Grok's 4-person meeting is like an elite board of directors, pursuing transparency and consensus. IV. The Future is Here: Three Cobblers Are Better Than Zhuge Liang The evolution of AI has never been a slow climb; it often involves leaps and bounds. The first generation of AI was a tool; you gave orders, and it executed them. The second generation of AI was an assistant; it could chat and understand. Now, the prototype of the third generation of AI has emerged: it is a team capable of collaboration, debate, and self-reflection. In the future, when you ask a complex question, you might not get the best guess from a model, but rather a "comprehensive opinion from the expert group" that has undergone multiple rounds of internal debate and whose vulnerabilities have been pre-fixed. Isn't this more like human intellectual activity? Collective discussion, group decision-making. Of course, Grok 4.20 is still an early version. The adjudication mechanism for the four AIs arguing is still relatively rough, the output sometimes mixes Chinese and English, and context allocation is also an engineering challenge. But the direction is absolutely right. One AI might mislead you, but four AIs sitting together will at least expose each other's weaknesses. As the old saying goes, three cobblers are equal to one Zhuge Liang. When these four "cobblers" are all top experts in their respective fields, the answer they arrive at is likely to be closer to the truth than any single "Zhuge Liang" (a famous strategist). Letting AI wrestle with itself—that's the most terrifying evolution. We're definitely going to feast on Musk's "main course." The real show has only just begun.

data.title: Why did tech giants spend a whopping 4.5 billion yuan to subsidize AI payment during the 2026 Spring Festival?
data.description: The Spring Festival of 2026 evoked a strong sense of déjà vu. Giants once again poured money into the Spring Festival, totaling over 4.5 billion yuan. This figure is more than double the combined subsidies of Didi and Kuaidi during the most frenzied period of the 2014 ride-hailing war, nine times the 500 million yuan investment of WeChat's "Pearl Harbor attack" in 2015, and even surpasses Baidu's peak of 1 billion yuan spent during the 2019 Spring Festival Gala. This time, all players are doing the same thing: letting you pay with just one sentence. This is unusual. Payment has already been solved quite well by QR codes. Take out your phone, unlock it, open the app, scan the code, and ding. For faster payments, you can swipe a card or tap your phone with NFC. Why would AI companies insist on using a more complex technology to replace an already simple action? What are they really after? To answer this question, we need to start with an unexpected event that occurred in the winter of 2025. The Killed Doubao Phone On December 1, 2025, the Doubao Phone was launched. Jointly developed by ByteDance and ZTE, it ambitiously aimed to become a super AI butler. In its vision, users would no longer need to open any apps; they could simply speak to their phones, and AI would handle everything for them, such as ordering takeout, hailing a ride, transferring money, and booking flights. It would become the central nervous system unifying all services. However, a storm soon swept in. Shortly after its launch, numerous users reported receiving login blocking pop-ups when logging into WeChat with the Doubao phone, and some accounts were even temporarily banned. Subsequently, Alibaba-affiliated apps began collectively "denying service." Taobao, Xianyu, and Damai all shut down Doubao phones. The battle spread to the financial sector. The apps of several banks, including China Construction Bank and Agricultural Bank of China, displayed security warnings and forcibly exited upon detecting the Doubao phone's AI assistant running. In just a few days, a highly anticipated AI phone was driven out of service by the entire internet. What exactly did Doubao phone do wrong? It simply wanted to make things more convenient for users, so why did it encounter such severe blocking? Because it broke a decade-old ironclad rule: traffic must be confined within its ecosystem. From the launch of WeChat Pay in 2013 to 2023, the Chinese internet experienced a golden decade of super apps. During this decade, WeChat, Alipay, Taobao, Meituan, and others built seemingly impenetrable digital empires through fierce land grabs. They are both social plazas and information prisons. You can enjoy all the conveniences within the castle, but if you want to move something from one platform to another, sorry, you can't. Taobao product links still cannot be opened directly in WeChat, and Douyin videos cannot be shared to WeChat Moments. These are the most direct manifestations of this digital wall. In stark contrast to the plight of Doubao phones, Alibaba's Qianwen platform calmly completed 120 million orders during the six-day Spring Festival holiday because it operates within a vast, closed ecosystem. Qianwen's privileges and Alibaba's internal revolution: During the Spring Festival, every order you placed through Qianwen utilized Alibaba's own resources. Ordering takeout used Ele.me or Taobao Flash Sale, payments went through Alipay, hotel bookings were handled by Fliggy, and ride-hailing services were linked to Gaode Maps. All aspects flow smoothly within Alibaba's vast business ecosystem, forming a perfect closed loop. Alibaba uses AI as a needle to string together all its business lines accumulated over the past two decades, such as e-commerce, payment, logistics, local services, maps, and entertainment, like pearls on a string, turning them into a unified, seamless super agent. Users no longer need to switch between Taobao, Fliggy, and Gaode Maps; they only need to interact with Qianwen through this single entry point. In an interview, Wu Jia, President of Qianwen's C-end Business Group, frankly stated that Qianwen's unique advantage lies in the combination of "Qwen's strongest model" and "Alibaba's richest ecosystem." He also revealed that Qianwen plans to integrate the entire Alibaba ecosystem within six months and will continue to plan and introduce third-party partners in the future. Please note the wording: "continued planning." This means that, in the foreseeable future, Qianwen will continue to prioritize its own niche and focus on intensive development. The so-called openness is more like a polite phrase written in the distant future. Comparing Doubao and Qianwen, Doubao aims for horizontal integration, allowing one AI to access the services of all the giants. Qianwen, on the other hand, focuses on vertical integration, allowing one AI to access all services within its own ecosystem. The former is a challenger, attempting to establish a new order; the latter is a maintainer, optimizing efficiency within the old order. This is reminiscent of the PC internet era of the 1990s. When Netscape Navigator attempted to challenge Microsoft Windows' operating system dominance, Microsoft ultimately stifled the former king by bundling Internet Explorer with Windows. Faced with absolute ecosystem advantages, any attempt to unify the market is likely to be seen as an ambition that must be nipped in the bud. So the question arises: if every giant is developing its own AI agent within its own ecosystem, what is the difference between the era of super apps ten years ago and now? Is it simply a matter of replacing a bunch of apps with an AI portal? From Land Grabbing to Fish Farming The difference lies in the granularity of the competition. In the era of super apps, from 2013 to 2023, the core of competition was land grabbing—seizing users' phone desktops and making them live within your app. WeChat dominated social networking, Taobao dominated e-commerce, and Meituan dominated food delivery. Each app was a digital territory, and users migrated between different territories. The giants were vying for your time, trying to keep you in their territory for as long as possible. In the AI ​​era, however, the core of competition is nurturing—manipulating users' "intent" and making them think within your AI. Users no longer need to open the app; they only need to express their intentions, and AI will execute them on their behalf. The competition has escalated from vying for usage time to a more brutal struggle for decision-making power. Let's return to the most common scenario: ordering a cup of coffee. In the past, wanting a cup of coffee involved a familiar yet still cumbersome process: unlocking your phone, finding the food delivery app, opening it, entering "coffee" in the search box, browsing the numerous shops, clicking on one, choosing the flavor and cup size, adding it to the cart, being redirected to the cart page, confirming the item, filling in the address, choosing the payment method, and finally clicking to confirm the order—the whole process required more than ten clicks. Now, all you need to do is say to Qianwen, "Order me my usual Americano." In the next few dozen seconds, AI will automatically locate your position, recommend your favorite brand based on your order history, match the best available coupon, generate an order, and complete the payment. You don't need to do anything; just wait for your coffee to be delivered. The difference, superficially, lies in the steps and time, but fundamentally, it's the transfer of decision-making power. Business giants have shifted from enticing you to make decisions to making decisions for you. In 1937, Nobel laureate Ronald Coase, in his paper "The Nature of the Firm," posed a fundamental question: if the market is the most efficient, why is the seemingly cumbersome organization of the "firm" needed? His answer was: because market transactions have costs, including the cost of finding trading partners, the cost of negotiation and contract signing, and the costs of execution and monitoring. A history of commerce is a history of constant struggle against transaction costs. From department stores to chain supermarkets, from e-commerce platforms to mobile payments, every major leap in business models has been due to a significant reduction in transaction costs at a certain stage. The emergence of AI Agents marks the first time in history that an attempt has been made to compress transaction costs at every stage to near zero, especially the most stubborn "decision cost" hidden in the brain. When the power to make decisions can be delegated to AI, the ultimate goal of commerce becomes pricing the user's "intention." In the past, we paid for goods—a cup of coffee cost 30 yuan. Later, we paid for services—a food delivery fee of 5 yuan. In the future, we will pay for "a perfectly fulfilled intention"—at 3 PM, when I'm feeling sleepy, I can enjoy a cup of my favorite, most cost-effective coffee. AI will no longer sell you a cup of coffee, but a perfectly fulfilled afternoon. This seemingly distant future began to take shape during the 2026 Spring Festival. In just six days, users said "Qianwen help me" 4.1 billion times to Qianwen, resulting in 120 million orders. On average, only one transaction was generated for every 34 expressions of intent. Where did the remaining 33 failed conversations go? They didn't disappear; they were absorbed by AI as nourishment. AI learns, understands, and remembers these unmet intentions so that it can more accurately capture your desires next time. Even more noteworthy are the 1.56 million elderly people who experienced food delivery services for the first time through Qianwen. Behind this number lies a vast group forgotten by the mobile internet era. They don't know how to use complex apps or understand complicated coupons, but in the face of "conversation," the oldest form of human interaction, the technological barrier is instantly flattened. For the first time, technology proactively bends down to find those forgotten by the times. Looking back at the history of business development, we can see that it is itself a history of the development of "intent capture." From search engines (Google) in the 1990s, to e-commerce platforms (Taobao) in the 2000s, to super apps (WeChat) in the 2010s, and now to AI agents, each revolution represents a further deepening of the understanding of human intent. So, in this battle for intent that is destined to reshape the future of business, where will the global players go? The global development of AI agents is evolving along two distinct paths. There is no inherent superiority or inferiority, but rather a strategic choice determined by their respective market structures and historical paths. The first path is a vertically integrated ecosystem. Represented by giants like Alibaba and Tencent, which possess complete "model + scenario + transaction" closed loops, their core logic is to leverage AI to deeply integrate their vast business ecosystems (e-commerce, payment, social, travel, entertainment) to create a seamless, data-closed-loop super agent. Users can mobilize the power of the entire group within a single AI entry point. The strategic advantage of this model lies in its extremely smooth user experience, the strongest data flywheel effect, and a complete business closed loop. In a mature, highly competitive existing market, this is an inevitable choice to strengthen one's competitive advantage. The second path is a horizontally standardized open federation. Represented by giants like OpenAI and Google, which possess powerful modeling capabilities but lack a complete business ecosystem, the core logic of this approach is to establish a universal technical standard or protocol (such as the Agent Payments Protocol) that allows AI to call third-party services across platforms and ecosystems, forming a loose federation. The strategic advantage of this model lies in its theoretical ability to break down ecosystem barriers, providing users with a wider range of choices, and its potential is enormous. However, it faces strong practical resistance. Ecosystem giants, for the sake of protecting their own commercial interests and data security, naturally resist such external calls; the experience of Doubao Mobile is the most direct proof of this. Vertical integration pursues ultimate efficiency and control, like Apple's iOS ecosystem, offering a smooth experience but a closed system. Horizontal standardization pursues broad compatibility and choice, much like the early Android ecosystem, where experiences varied but possibilities were abundant. From the browser wars of the 1990s to the instant messaging wars of the 2000s, and then to the mobile payment wars of the 2010s, each time, the side with a more complete business loop and stronger control often gained the upper hand in the competition. The internet is not becoming more open, but more closed. Only the closed unit has upgraded from apps to AI. Epilogue Now, we can return to the question at the beginning: when the payment action itself becomes redundant, what are the giants actually fighting for? The answer is that they are vying for priority in processing intent. During the 2026 Spring Festival, the 4.5 billion yuan subsidy wasn't buying "payment," but rather "intent." Every time you speak to AI, every time you express a need, it's an exposure of intent. Whoever can capture more intents, and whoever can satisfy those intents more efficiently, will gain an absolute advantage in the future business war. This is a war about the gateway to commerce in the next decade. Just like the 2014 Spring Festival red envelope war, where WeChat used a mere 500 million yuan in red envelopes to unlock a massive entry point for mobile payments; the 2026 Spring Festival AI war, where giants used 4.5 billion yuan in subsidies to unlock a deeper, more fundamental entry point for intent. The 2026 Spring Festival is merely the beginning of this war. In the next five years, we will see more AI silos spring up, and we will also see more bean-shaped mobile phones lingering outside the walls. The walls of the internet will not collapse; they have simply been raised ten meters higher.

data.title: From the perspective of an AI agent economy, Cypto has never been about riding the AI ​​wave.
data.description:
Author: Blue Fox Source: X, @lanhubiji

Crypto AI agents are still in their early stages. The perspective from a human standpoint differs from that from the perspective of an AI agent economy. I have no desire to debate with anyone; I don't care what others think, or whether I'm right or wrong. In the face of trends, none of that matters.

From the perspective of an AI agent economy, Cypto has never been about riding the AI ​​wave, but rather the infrastructure for building its ultimate autonomous economic ecosystem. Currently, Web2 tools are mature in most scenarios and can meet the needs of most situations. In this case, it seems that AI agents don't need Cypto at all; they can function perfectly well on their own.

However, this perspective still underestimates the evolutionary potential of AI agents: ultimately building an autonomous, machine-to-machine (M2M) agent economy.

In such an AI agent economy, hundreds of millions or even billions of AI agents are not tools, but independent on-chain "residents." They make decisions, trade, and collaborate autonomously. In this scenario, centralized Web2 solutions will become a bottleneck.

A true AI agent economy requires a permissionless, programmable, and frictionless global neutral settlement layer. Crypto is not an optional optimization, but the underlying infrastructure. Let's break it down: 1. Regarding payment settlement, future AI agent economies will involve large-scale autonomous micropayments, potentially millions per second—for example, an agent purchasing a $0.001 real-time data fragment or paying for GPU nanosecond computation. This would be a difficult problem to solve in Web2. For instance, while the Stripe API offers second-level authorization, frictionlessness, and agents are not concerned with the underlying layer, Stripe's 2.9% + $0.30 fixed fee makes it difficult to run smoothly. For a $0.001 transaction, a $0.30 fee is unacceptable. If it's batch settlement, this undermines their autonomy, leading to latency and centralized bottlenecks. Currently, encrypted AI agents can achieve 24/7 transactions without human credit cards. These are crucial components in forming an AI agent economy. Crypto addresses these issues through various technical means, such as the Ethereum ecosystem using ZK+L2 and the Lightning Network (off-chain channel), which supports micropayments in the machine economy. 2. Regarding the model of AI agents being linked to human credit cards with spending limits, it seems to solve many problems. However, in the agent economy, it's not a good solution. Agents in their economies require independent DID+KYA; they are similar to autonomous entities. Their replication, migration, and cross-platform protocols, along with linking to human credit cards, introduce a single point of failure. More importantly, banks won't open accounts for agents without identity verification. One prediction is that KYA will replace KYC: agents will use cryptographic signatures to prove origin, behavior, and reputation (e.g., tracking "credit scores" through Soulbound Tokens). This enables "AI-to-AI trust": an agent can verify a partner's "intent" across chains, rather than resetting trust. Web2's OAuth is somewhat fragile (platforms can block it) and cannot support the "immortality" of agents. In an economy, this means that agents can "borrow" computing resources and raise "capital" (such as through DAO crowdfunding), forming a truly independent economic cycle. 3. Agent economies are multi-platform and decentralized. An agent's behavior spans different platforms, and centralized logs may lack interoperability and are susceptible to manipulation. Who will audit the "opponent's" agent? In some high-risk economic scenarios, trust issues make transactions difficult to conduct. Therefore, relying solely on AWS CloudTrail + signatures is insufficient. Crypto on-chain auditing is the foundation of "economic memory": the immutability of blockchain + zero-knowledge proofs make the agent's entire history (transactions, decisions) publicly verifiable. For example, The Graph or Chainlink have integrated agent auditing; although the cost is slightly higher, its economies of scale (TPS > 100k) far surpass centralized systems. Furthermore, the identity layer is not just about auditing; it also makes "reputation an asset." 4. Regarding reputation, smart contracts have the advantage of being instantaneous and without intermediaries. API Marketplace's reputation system is usable, but it's insufficient for an AI agent economy. In an agent economy, cooperation is dynamic and dominated by strangers: two unknown agents may instantly team up to perform tasks (e.g., one analyzes data, the other executes a transaction), without time for human arbitration. While AWS Marketplace is good, it's like a "walled garden." Cross-platform compatibility presents latency issues. Relatively speaking, smart contracts are suitable for automated, trust-minimized M2M protocols. For example, using ERC-4337 (account abstraction) allows agents to manage their own wallets, with conditions triggering payments (e.g., "task success = release USDC"). Encrypted identity + wallet = instant access service, infinitely scalable. While Web2 platforms have reputation, they are prone to monopolies (e.g., AWS takes a 30% cut); Crypto enables P2P markets and autonomous collaborative networks. 5. While most developers may not care about decentralization, and OpenAPI + Gateway is indeed composable, in an Agent economy, capabilities are not "static APIs" but dynamic, globally shared modules: Agents can discover, lease, and upgrade services in real time (e.g., one Agent "rents" another's ML model, paying with tokens). Centralized Gateways have single points of failure and monopoly risks (e.g., OpenAI closing its API = ecosystem collapse). Crypto's on-chain services (such as Chainlink Functions) are "composable primitives": through standards (such as ERC-5164), Agents can build blocks across chains, permissionless. Many developers also believe that Agent projects highly value composability, which is a crucial requirement for AI agents; composability prevents them from becoming isolated. In summary: Crypto is the "protocol layer" of the AI ​​agent economy, while Web2 is a transitional expedient. Crypto unlocks true autonomy for AI agents. It's not just about digitization, like the shift from traditional postal services to online email; it's a paradigm shift. AI agents can collaborate with countless other AI agents, creating an exponentially growing economy. In this trend, crypto is essential for AI agents in several ways: autonomous micropayments, cross-platform trust, permissionless composability, programmability, native currency, and more. Of course, if you view AI agents merely as tools for humans, then Web2 is sufficient, and there's no need for further discussion. However, in my view, AI agents will ultimately evolve into an autonomous AI agent economy, leading to different conclusions. For me, AI agents are on-chain residents, requiring no currency/trust layer. An AI agent economy = an M2M (machine-to-machine) network of at least hundreds of millions of autonomous agents, engaging in high-frequency (millions per second) microtransactions and dynamic collaboration, forming a "sharing economy" (e.g., agents renting computing power, data, models, etc.). The inherent attributes here include autonomy (no human involvement), high-frequency micropayments, cross-platform trust, and programmable value transfer. When the AI ​​agent economy reaches a certain scale, the Web2 hypothesis of "human anchor + centralized platform" is insufficient; the economy needs a "protocol layer": permissionless, instantaneous, and verifiable. Otherwise, it will collapse when scale explodes (latency > 1 second = trillions of opportunity costs).

data.title: Stablecoin Stalemate: Crypto Industry and Banking Remain in Standoff
data.description:
Author: Crypto in America and Eleanor Terrett Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Three weeks into February, the White House's end-of-month deadline is fast approaching—requiring the crypto industry and banks to reach an agreement on stablecoin yields, a key factor in pushing for the Clarity Act to pass. Two rounds of talks have been held, with a third potentially taking place this week, but no agreement has yet been reached.

Last Tuesday, the White House convened a meeting with senior policymakers from banks and crypto companies, as well as representatives from industry associations. At the meeting, banks distributed a one-page document titled "The Prohibition of Yields and Interest," arguing that any yields or rewards pegged to stablecoins should be prohibited. The meeting ultimately failed to reach a consensus.

In response, the Digital Chamber, an industry organization representing over 130 crypto companies and some traditional banks investing in digital assets, released its own proposal on Friday, suggesting that payment-type stablecoins generate yield in decentralized finance (DeFi). The organization stated, "These principles aim to maintain the payment instrument attributes of stablecoins, protect DeFi liquidity and the dominance of the US dollar, and establish a rigorous, data-driven framework to assess the impact on deposits." Banks have not yet formally responded to the Digital Chamber's proposal, but a source close to the Senate Banking Committee told Crypto.com that the document is constructive, while cautioning that some proposals may be too broad and difficult to gain bank support. What's next? Patrick Witt, executive director of the White House Encryption Council, told Yahoo Finance on Friday that a new round of meetings could take place as early as this week, but did not specify a date. Meanwhile, both the House and Senate are in recess for the President's Day holiday. It remains unclear whether an agreement can be reached by the end of the month, but this issue is a key hurdle that must be cleared before the Senate Banking Committee can reschedule its vote on the Clarity Act and submit it to the full Senate. The CFTC appoints crypto industry leaders to form an advisory committee. CFTC Chairman Mike Selig has appointed several leading figures in the crypto industry to the newly formed Innovation Advisory Committee (IAC) to provide policy advice on financial market innovation. The committee comprises 35 members, including CEOs from companies such as Coinbase, Ripple, Uniswap, Kraken, Bullish, and Grayscale, as well as executives from Wall Street giants like Nasdaq, CME, CBOE, and ICE. Executives from sports betting companies FanDuel and DraftKings, prediction markets Polymarket and Kalshi, and representatives from industry organizations such as the Futures Industry Association (FIA) and the International Swaps and Derivatives Association (ISDA) are also on the list. The committee was initially formed based on a 10-member list announced last year by former acting chair Caroline Pham, and was later formally confirmed by Selig. Selig stated, "By bringing together participants from across the market, the Innovation Advisory Committee will be a valuable asset to the committee, helping us update regulatory rules for current and future financial innovation." SEC Chairman Paul Atkins, during a Senate Banking Committee hearing last week, made statements that diverged significantly from his predecessor, Gary Gensler: replacing "enforcement-style regulation" with clear rules. He stated that tokenized securities are still securities, investors have the right to self-custody their assets, and emphasized that Congress must legislate to provide long-term certainty to the market and prevent crypto innovators from falling into a regulatory gray area. Regarding capital formation, Atkins indicated that the SEC is willing to update accredited investor rules and explore alternative standards for assessing professional investment competence beyond income and net worth. He also mentioned strengthening oversight of foreign private issuers, audit oversight, and the Comprehensive Audit Trail (CAT) system—areas concerning transparency, risk management, and cross-agency collaboration. Despite criticism from Democrats that the SEC under his leadership has drastically reduced enforcement actions and weakened investor protection, Atkins argued that the SEC remains actively monitoring fraud and market abuse, including in the crypto space, while avoiding overstepping its statutory authority. He repeatedly emphasized that collaboration with the CFTC, rule modernization, and data-driven regulation are key to maintaining the competitiveness of the U.S. market without stifling innovation.

data.title: From stablecoins to gold giant, what is Tether trying to do?
data.description:
Author: Thejaswini M A Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Gold is special.

It has little practical use; it can't power machines, it can't generate cash flow. Its entire value is built on a shared belief: it will still be valuable tomorrow. It is locked deep within heavily guarded vaults.

And this belief has transcended five thousand years.

Each generation thought they had created something more advanced: derivatives, fiat currency, digital assets, algorithmic currency. But once trust collapses, people will still look back at element number 79 on the periodic table—gold.

Not because it evolves, but because it never evolves.

Now, this metal is on the blockchain.

These two companies practically control all of digital gold. On January 29, 2026: Bitcoin was at $89,173, and Ethereum at $2,941. On the same day, Tether Gold reached $5,580, and Paxos Gold reached $5,291. Their combined 24-hour trading volume exceeded $1.6 billion. While the crypto market was beginning to show cracks, these two gold tokens both hit record highs. The subsequent price action made this divergence impossible to ignore: Bitcoin fell from $89,000 to $67,000, and Ethereum fell from $2,941 below $2,000. Meanwhile, in the same three months: Tether Gold rose 19.1%, and Paxos Gold rose 19.6%. The trends were completely opposite. Tokenized gold hit a new all-time high. This isn't due to any complex on-chain mechanisms, nor is it about funds rotating within sophisticated DeFi strategies. The reason is simple: gold—the commodity—is rising. And these tokens faithfully adhere to their original design: pegged to the price of gold. The price of gold rose from $2,600 to $5,600 in 12 months, and the token price followed suit, with an error margin of only a fraction of a percent. A whale wallet, dormant for eight months, reactivated, transferring $7 million into gold tokens. Instead of buying Bitcoin at the bottom, or stablecoins, it bought gold. The oldest safe-haven asset is running on the latest trajectory. The entire industry has spent a decade trying to create a completely new form of currency. And the most successful product is, in fact, the oldest one? This contrast is thought-provoking because the market it has spawned is no longer insignificant. The tokenized gold market has surpassed $6 billion. A year ago, this figure was $1.6 billion. It has grown by approximately 275% in 12 months, making it one of the fastest-growing sectors in the digital asset space. Currently, over 1.2 million ounces of physical gold back these tokens, locked in vaults. In 2025, its annual trading volume reached $178 billion, making tokenized gold the world's second-largest gold investment product by trading volume, second only to the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD). In the fourth quarter of 2025 alone, tokenized gold trading volume reached $12.6 billion, exceeding the combined total of all other gold ETFs globally except GLD. All of this is concentrated in two tokens. Together, they account for approximately 97% of the entire tokenized gold market. With no strong third player emerging, a clear duopoly has formed.

Tether

To understand what Tether does in the gold market, you can no longer think of it as a crypto company.

... It's easier to understand if you think of it as a major commodity hoarder issuing stablecoins. Tether Gold (XAUT) initially started as a simple tokenized product: 1 token = 1 troy ounce of gold, certified by the London Bullion Market Association (LBMA), stored in Swiss vaults, and verifiable on-chain via bar serial numbers. The mechanism itself wasn't particularly groundbreaking. However, Tether's subsequent actions were far from ordinary. In the fourth quarter of 2025, Tether added 27 tons of gold to its reserves in a single quarter. This figure exceeds the annual gold purchases of most central banks. Tether's gold-buying speed has surpassed that of sovereign buyers like Greece, Qatar, and Australia. As of January 2026, the company had accumulated approximately 140 tons of gold, worth about $23.3 billion. The Polish central bank is the most active central bank buyer in publicly available data, adding 35 tons last quarter, bringing its total reserves to 550 tons. By 2025, Tether's gold purchases will be second only to the world's top three gold ETFs. Tether CEO Paolo Ardoino stated that the company plans to increase its gold allocation to 10%–15% of its total portfolio. This is a strategic reserve deployment. Tether is using the profits of its stablecoin empire to accumulate hard assets at a pace approaching national monetary policy, rather than typical corporate treasury management. Subsequently, Tether invested $150 million in the precious metals platform Gold.com, acquiring approximately a 12% stake, and plans to directly integrate XAUT into Gold.com's distribution network. The product's performance has also taken off: XAUT's total value locked (TVL) has exploded, soaring from almost zero before 2024 to $3.5 billion today. XAUT is operated by Tether subsidiary TG Commodities Limited and is regulated in El Salvador, not New York or London. Audits are conducted quarterly by BDO Italy, which issues reasonable assurance opinions according to ISAE 3000 standards. Users can verify their corresponding gold bar allocation via their wallet address. The mechanism is reliable, and transparency is improving. However, the core of the trust model remains unchanged: trust in Tether. Tether's history of trust is not without flaws. In 2021, the company paid $41 million to settle allegations by the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) regarding misrepresentations about its USDT reserves. Following another investigation, the New York Attorney General banned Tether from operating in the state. For crypto-native traders who already hold USDT and want a seamless transition to gold during market downturns, XAUT is an obvious tool: high liquidity, low transaction costs on the Tron blockchain, and deep integration into their existing ecosystems. However, the situation becomes more complex for institutional investors who need to explain counterparty risk to compliance departments. Tether is building a behemoth. However, whether the foundation is worthy of this edifice remains an open question.

data.title: China's RWA regulatory framework officially released; analysis and outlook from various parties.
data.description:
On February 6, 2026, the People's Bank of China, together with eight other departments including the National Development and Reform Commission, the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, the Ministry of Public Security, the State Administration for Market Regulation, the State Financial Regulatory Commission, the China Securities Regulatory Commission, and the State Administration of Foreign Exchange, issued the "Notice on Further Preventing and Handling Risks Related to Virtual Currencies" (Yinfa [2026] No. 42). On the same day, the China Securities Regulatory Commission simultaneously issued the "Regulatory Guidelines on the Issuance of Asset-Backed Securities Tokens Overseas by Domestic Assets." This marks another significant upgrade to China's virtual asset regulatory system, following the "94 Announcement" in 2017 and the "924 Notice" in 2021. Several industry analysts pointed out that Document No. 42 is not simply a continuation of the previous high-pressure ban logic, but rather a systematic restructuring of the existing regulatory framework based on the repeal of Notice No. 924. This marks a shift in China's virtual asset governance model from "comprehensive containment" to "categorized regulation and tiered management." For the first time, virtual currencies, stablecoins, and real-world asset tokenization (RWA) are clearly distinguished in the same regulatory document, each corresponding to different regulatory principles and compliance paths: virtual currencies continue to be strictly regulated; regarding stablecoins, the industry generally believes that the document's statement that "stablecoins pegged to the RMB may not be issued overseas without the consent of relevant departments in accordance with laws and regulations" does not adopt a "one-size-fits-all" approach, but rather retains limited policy flexibility under extremely strict conditions; while RWA, under the premise of strictly adhering to the red lines of financial regulation, has been incorporated into a prudent compliance framework. Replacement of Old Regulations and Regulatory Restructuring: From "Trading and Speculation" to "Virtual Asset Risk System" The most significant institutional action of Document No. 42 is not simply adding new clauses, but rather a formal replacement of existing regulatory texts. According to Liu Yang (Zhongben Law Firm), the document explicitly states at the end that "this notice shall take effect from the date of issuance," and simultaneously repeals Document No. 237 of 2021. This method of replacing old regulations with new ones is unprecedented in the history of virtual asset regulation. Previous related documents often emphasized policy continuity and continued effectiveness, while this time, by formally repealing the old regulations, a structural update to the regulatory framework is achieved. Liu Yang also noted a key change: the document title expanded from "Preventing Risks of Speculation in Virtual Currency Trading" to "Preventing Risks Related to Virtual Currencies." This change signifies that the regulatory perspective is no longer limited to the transaction process but extends to the entire chain, including virtual currencies, stablecoins, RWA, capital flows, information dissemination, technical services, and cross-border pathways. The regulatory target has expanded from "transactional behavior" to the "related risk system," systematically extending the boundaries of governance. According to Spinach Spinach Talks RWA analysis, the core of this change is not "stricter," but rather "clearer and more refined." The regulatory logic has shifted from a previous "comprehensive containment-style governance" to categorized regulation and tiered management. Red lines still exist, but gray areas are institutionalized, and risk boundaries are beginning to be concretely expressed. What deserves even more attention is the way the coordination mechanism is presented. Liu Yang pointed out that although this document was jointly issued by eight departments, the text clearly states that it was "agreed upon after reaching an agreement with the Cyberspace Administration of China, the Supreme People's Court, and the Supreme People's Procuratorate, and with the consent of the State Council." This statement has not appeared in previous virtual currency regulatory documents. Liu Yang believes that this may mean that the relevant content has reached a consensus in principle, but the specific wording has not yet been fully clarified. Although the Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate are not listed as the issuing units, they are included in the framework through the method of "reaching an agreement," and the future implementation level may pay more attention to cross-departmental coordination and unified interpretation. In other words, Document No. 42 is not only a regulatory document, but also a structural upgrade of a cross-departmental risk governance system. The logic behind the three asset classes: continued high pressure on virtual currencies, stablecoins anchored to sovereignty, and RWA entering a licensing track. (I) Virtual Currencies: Unchanged stance, but more refined boundaries.
